race.name: Tour de Palm Springs
race.tagline: Desert charity century through the Coachella Valley. 28 years. 10,000 riders. 20 bands. $5 million raised. Wind, sun, and a post-ride party that rivals the ride itself.

vitals.field_size: 6,000-10,000 riders annually from 46 states and 4 countries (all distances)
vitals.registration: Online via tourdepalmsprings.com or BikeReg. Military/veteran discount available. On-site registration adds $10.
vitals.entry_fee: Varies by distance. Military/veteran discount to $100 for the 103-mile route. On-site registration adds $10.
vitals.feed_zones: SAG stops approximately every 20 miles with bike mechanics, first aid, live bands, hot pasta, wings, energy drinks, and refreshments. 20+ SAG vehicles and RACES HAM radio operators on course. 2,500 volunteers.
vitals.cutoff_time: Generous. SAG support ensures all riders can complete their chosen distance.

climate.primary: Southern California desert — mild February mornings, warm afternoons, and unpredictable wind
climate.description: February in Palm Springs delivers near-ideal cycling temperatures — morning lows around 45-55F warming to pleasant 65-75F afternoons with low humidity, clear skies, and abundant sunshine. The Coachella Valley sits in a desert basin surrounded by mountains, which means wind is the defining weather variable. Riders have reported calm, perfect mornings and also 40 mph headwinds with 60 mph gusts that sandblast exposed stretches near the dunes. Rain probability is low (~12%). The desert UV is intense even at moderate temperatures.
challenges: Wind is the wildcard — Coachella Valley wind can reach 40+ mph with sandblasting gusts near the dunes
Desert sun exposure across open roads with minimal shade
Dry air requires proactive hydration despite comfortable temperatures
Morning starts can be cool (45-55F) — arm warmers needed early, shed by midday
Crosswinds on exposed desert stretches between cities

terrain.primary: Flat to gently rolling desert roads through the Coachella Valley
terrain.surface: All paved roads. The century route starts in downtown Palm Springs, heads north toward Twentynine Palms on gradual ascending grades, follows Dillon Road through the desert into Indio, then returns northwest through La Quinta, Palm Desert, and Cathedral City back to Palm Springs. Rolling hills and gradual climbs but nothing steep. The terrain is universally accessible — designed for mass participation across all ability levels.
features: Flat to gently rolling — ~2,700 ft of climbing across 102 miles
Desert scenery — San Jacinto Mountains, Santa Rosa Mountains, Coachella Valley panoramas
Paved roads throughout — no technical demands
Route passes through multiple Coachella Valley cities
Wind exposure on open desert stretches between towns

fondo_rating.scoring_notes: Migrated from gravel-race-automation. Original score: 54. adventure dimension dropped, road_surface added. Score recalculated: 53.

course_description.character: A 102-mile loop through the Coachella Valley on flat to gently rolling paved roads. The century route starts in downtown Palm Springs, heads north toward Twentynine Palms on gradual ascending grades, follows Dillon Road through open desert into Indio, then returns northwest through La Quinta, Palm Desert, Cathedral City, and back to Palm Springs. Five SAG stops at approximately miles 17, 42, 57, 68, and 90 — each staffed with mechanics, first aid, live bands, and hot food. The terrain is flat desert with gentle grades and occasional rollers. The challenge is distance and wind, not elevation.
course_description.signature_challenge: Wind. The Coachella Valley sits in a natural wind corridor between the San Jacinto and San Gorgonio mountain passes. On calm February mornings, the ride is a desert paradise. When the wind arrives — and it can arrive without warning — riders face 40 mph headwinds and sandblasting gusts that turn a flat century into a grueling endurance test. The Dillon Road section is the most exposed and most notorious. You cannot train for this wind. You can only prepare mentally and ride through it.
suffering_zones: Downtown Palm Springs — The Mass Start
La Quinta to Palm Desert — The Suburban Return
Cathedral City to Palm Springs — The Home Stretch

logistics.airport: Palm Springs International Airport (PSP) — 10 minutes from downtown Palm Springs start. Also reachable from Ontario (ONT, 90 min) or LAX (2.5 hours).
logistics.lodging_strategy: Single-day event — book one or two nights in Palm Springs or neighboring Coachella Valley cities. February is shoulder season for the resort crowd, so hotel rates are reasonable. Downtown Palm Springs puts you walking distance from the start. Cathedral City, Rancho Mirage, and Palm Desert offer budget-friendly alternatives 10-20 minutes away.
logistics.food: SAG stops every ~20 miles serve hot pasta, wings, energy drinks, water, and snacks. Post-ride festival includes beer garden and vendor expo with food options. Palm Springs and the Coachella Valley have abundant dining from casual to upscale. Pre-ride dinner: downtown Palm Springs has dozens of restaurants on Palm Canyon Drive.

history.origin_story: On January 31, 1998, Tim Esser — president of Esser Air Conditioning and Heating in Palm Springs — had an idea for a charity bike ride in the desert. He envisioned routes of 5, 15, 25, and 50 miles to raise money for local nonprofits. The first event, originally called 'Spokes for Different Folks,' drew 400 cyclists. Tim and his mother Fran formed a nonprofit called CVSPIN (Coachella Valley Serving People In Need), and the first official Tour de Palm Springs was held in 1999 with 1,000 riders. From that modest beginning, the event grew into one of the largest charity cycling events in America — now attracting up to 10,000 riders from 46 states and 4 countries, supported by 2,500 volunteers, and raising nearly $5 million for over 150 Coachella Valley nonprofits. Esser Air Conditioning remains the title sponsor 28 years later.
notable_moments: 1998: Tim Esser launches 'Spokes for Different Folks' with 400 riders
1999: Rebranded as Tour de Palm Springs under the newly formed CVSPIN nonprofit. 1,000 riders participate.
2000s: Event grows steadily, adding distance options and entertainment along the route
2010s: Event matures into one of America's largest charity cycling events, attracting 6,000-10,000 riders annually
2023: 25th Annual Tour de Palm Springs honors first responders and veterans
2025: 27th Annual event, February 8. Continued growth and community impact.
2026: 28th Annual event, February 7. Honorary guest Frankie Andreu (9x Tour de France, 2x Olympian). Cumulative fundraising approaches $5 million for 150+ nonprofits.
history.reputation: The Coachella Valley's annual cycling celebration. Not a race — a rolling desert festival that raises millions for local nonprofits while putting 10,000 riders on the road with 20 bands, 2,500 volunteers, and February sunshine.

biased_opinion.verdict: The Desert Block Party on Wheels
biased_opinion.summary: Tour de Palm Springs is what happens when a guy named Tim Esser has an idea about bikes and charity in the California desert, and then spends 28 years turning that idea into one of the largest cycling events in America. Starting with 400 riders in 1998 as 'Spokes for Different Folks,' the event now draws up to 10,000 cyclists from 46 states and 4 countries, with 2,500 volunteers and 20+ bands turning the Coachella Valley into a rolling festival. The cycling itself is straightforward — flat desert roads, gentle grades, zero technical demands — but the experience is anything but ordinary. Live music at every SAG stop. Cheerleaders. A marching band. Hot pasta and wings mid-ride. A post-ride party with a beer garden and vendor expo. Nearly $5 million raised for 150+ local nonprofits. This is not a race. It is a desert celebration that happens to involve bicycles.
strengths: Festival atmosphere: 20+ bands on course and at the finish, cheerleaders, marching band, beer garden, vendor expo. No other charity ride in America puts this much entertainment on the route.
Charitable impact: Nearly $5 million raised for 150+ Coachella Valley nonprofits through CVSPIN since 1998. Military, veteran, and first-responder honors.
Scale and accessibility: 6,000-10,000 riders, 8 distance options from 1-mile walk to 102-mile century. Everyone from your kid to your grandparent can participate.
February desert weather: When most of the country is buried in winter, Palm Springs delivers 65-75F sunshine. The timing is the event's greatest asset.
Volunteer army: 2,500 volunteers ensure SAG stops are stocked, roads are safe, and riders are supported every 20 miles.
weaknesses: Wind is the wildcard: Coachella Valley wind can turn a pleasant desert cruise into a sandblasting survival march. Riders have reported 40 mph headwinds with 60 mph gusts.
No competitive element: Zero timing, zero racing, zero results. If you want to test yourself against others, this is not your event.
Suburban route: The course passes through a chain of desert cities connected by commercial corridors — pleasant but not backcountry adventure.
Flat terrain: 2,700 feet over 102 miles will not challenge strong cyclists. The distance is the only physical test.
Regional identity: Well-known in Southern California but not nationally recognized as a premier cycling event.
biased_opinion.bottom_line: Tour de Palm Springs is the ride you do when you want to combine 100 miles of desert cycling with a music festival and a charitable mission. The terrain will not challenge you. The wind might destroy you. The experience will surprise you. Come for the cause, stay for the bands, and leave knowing your entry fee helped 150 nonprofits in the Coachella Valley.

final_verdict.one_liner: America's most festive charity century. Flat desert roads, 20 bands, 10,000 riders, and $5 million raised for the Coachella Valley.
final_verdict.should_you_race: You do not race Tour de Palm Springs — you ride it, you eat hot pasta at mile 42, and you dance to live music at every SAG stop. Yes if you want a fully supported century in perfect February weather with a festival atmosphere that no other charity ride matches. Yes if you want to escape winter and ride through the Coachella Valley desert with 10,000 other cyclists. Yes if you want your registration fee to benefit 150+ local nonprofits. Skip if you want competitive timing, challenging terrain, or backcountry adventure — this is a celebration, not a suffer-fest.
final_verdict.alternatives: If you want a bigger desert century with more terrain: El Tour de Tucson (100 miles, November, Sonoran Desert). If you want a more prestigious charity ride: Pan-Mass Challenge (192 miles, $6,000 fundraising minimum, Dana-Farber Cancer Institute). If you want a massive community ride with Southern California scenery: AIDS/LifeCycle (545 miles, San Francisco to Los Angeles). If you want a flat desert ride without the charity element: America's Most Beautiful Bike Ride (100 miles, Lake Tahoe).

videos.title: Tour de Palm Springs 2023 (Century Ride)
videos.description:
The Tour de Palm Springs is one of America's largest annual festive cycling charity events in America. This year, I joined the event with a buddy to ride the 102 mile route with incredible SAG support to get us through the course.

Thanks @ljapson777 for donating the footage of me riding! 

#tourdepalmsprings
videos.curation_reason: Century ride recap with good view count and focuses on the 102-mile route experience.

videos.title: Tour De Palm Springs 2026..Was it worth it?
videos.description:
I came back to Tour de Palm Springs 100 for the third time.. and this year, they finally brought back the REAL route.

This video was filmed entirely on the Insta360 GO Ultra, capturing the energy of the start, desert miles and brutal wind.

if you are thinking about riding Tour de Palm Springs, training for your first century ride or just love immersive POV cycling content - this one is for you.

Check out my ride on Strava: https://strava.app.link/TV9STx4wC0b

***CORRECTION: the 2nd SAG stop was at Westside Elementary School not Coachella Valley HS. The band playing was from the High School.***

Let me know in the comments, would you ride Tour de Palm Springs every year?

#tourdepalmsprings
#centuryride 
#cycling 
#granfondo 
#povcycling 
#biking

videos.title: 【4K HDR】 Tour de Palm Springs 2022
videos.description:
Tour de Palm Springs 2022 - Century Ride

Tour de Palm Springs
https://tourdepalmsprings.com/about-us/history/
The Tour de Palm Springs has raised over $4,000,000 for Coachella Valley charities since 1999.

Tim Esser had an idea on January 31, 1998. He would hold a bike event in Palm Springs that would allow local cyclist to ride 5, 15, 25 and 50 miles. 400 cyclists rode in the first event, known at that time as Spokes for Different Folks. Tim, however, being the guy that never goes only part way, decided to use the event as a launch pad for his own personal ride across America. It took him 30 days to cross the country from Palm Springs, California to Jacksonville, Florida. When it was all over Tim had survived his chase vehicle crashing and catching on fire, a fall that sprained his shoulder, rain in the valleys, snow in the mountains and tornadoes on the plains! But, he made it.

Mother Son Tour de Palm RidersThe first event raised $20,000 and was hailed as a great success by all who participated. Tim, along with his mother Fran, set up a not for profit organization called CVSPIN. Even though many cyclists may think that CVSPIN means to turn over their bicycle pedals as fast as possible in the Coachella Valley, it actually stands for Coachella Valley Serving People In Need. The first Tour de Palm Springs was held in 1999. The number of riders exceeded 1,000 and a 100 mile (century) ride was added. In 2001, the Coachella Valley Desert Business Association recognized the Tour de Palm Springs as the best event in the Valley. The competition included the Bob Hope PGA Tournament, the Dinah Shore LPGA Tournament, The Skins Game and the Tennis Masters Series. Every year the Tour de Palm Springs continues to attract more and more riders. The money generated has gone to help many organizations in the Coachella Valley. In 2015 over $179,000 was donated to 80 local charities that in their own special ways met the varied needs of the people in the Coachella Valley.

#TourdePalmSprings #100milechallenge #BikeTour
#로드바이크 #팜스프링
#LA_Walkers

quotes.text: They closed Dylan Road for this event. Following this big group got a draft. They're fast. Back to Dylan Road, man. I love Dylan Road. The rolling. It's open road. There's no cars. So, took advantage of it.
There's a band playing at Fantasy Springs SAG station. A lot of bikers. There's a lot of food here, guys. There are kids asking to hold our bikes. I had pasta. I had a tamale. I had banana.

key_challenges.name: Dylan Road
key_challenges.description: Rolling road section that riders describe as both challenging and enjoyable. This road is closed specifically for the event, allowing riders to take advantage of no car traffic and higher speeds.

key_challenges.name: Trail Avenue 52
key_challenges.description: Section with significant headwind that riders encounter later in the route.

key_challenges.name: Mile 85-90 stretch
key_challenges.description: The final challenging section where riders report feeling fatigued and that their legs are done.

terrain_notes.text: Features newly paved sections with no car traffic on closed roads, allowing riders to maintain higher speeds around 40 mph on some segments.

race_day_tips.text: Start early - riders wake up at 3:00 AM for the event. Take advantage of the closed roads like Dylan Road where you can maintain higher speeds without car traffic.
Utilize the Fantasy Springs aid station at mile 38 which offers substantial food options including pasta, tamales, bananas, oranges, and popcorn.

additional_quotes.text: There are kids asking to hold our bikes. Bike holders. A lot of food here, guys. There's bananas. Orange. Popcorn.
Was it worth it this year? Yes. Are we going to come back next year? I think so if they kept this route.

rider_intel.search_text: Tour de Palm Springs is a 102-mile desert ride that has riders waking up at 3:00 AM for an early start. The route features closed roads including Dylan Road, which offers rolling terrain and allows speeds around 40 mph without car traffic. A major aid station at Fantasy Springs at mile 38 provides extensive food options including pasta, tamales, bananas, and popcorn, with local kids helping hold bikes. The course includes newly paved sections and a new route addition. Key challenges include headwinds on Trail Avenue 52 and the final stretch from miles 85-90 where fatigue sets in significantly. The event has good community support with bands playing at aid stations and enthusiastic volunteers throughout the desert course.
